# Overview

This document finds, downloads, and cleans NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation
Index) data for a small study area in southeastern Arizona from the earliest
reliable satellite record through 2025.

## Study Area

The bounding box is defined by two corners:

| Corner | Latitude  | Longitude   |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| NE     | 31.939848 | −109.077395 |
| SW     | 31.935964 | −109.083181 |

The box spans roughly **580 m (E–W) × 440 m (N–S)**, placing it near the
Peloncillo Mountains straddling the Arizona/New Mexico border. Vegetation in
this Sky Island region transitions from Chihuahuan Desert scrub at lower
elevations to oak woodland — making long-term NDVI trends ecologically
informative.

## Language Rationale

> **Why Python here?**
> Google Earth Engine (GEE) has a mature, well-documented Python client (`earthengine-api`)
> that gives direct access to petabytes of analysis-ready satellite imagery —
> Landsat, MODIS, Sentinel, and others — without downloading raw rasters. The
> Python GEE API is the industry standard for large-scale remote sensing time
> series extraction, and `geemap` extends it with helpful utilities. Tasks that
> require *talking to the satellite archive* stay in Python.

> **Why R here?**
> Once the time series data land as a local CSV, R's `tidyverse` ecosystem
> (especially `dplyr`, `lubridate`, and `ggplot2`) offers expressive, readable
> pipelines for data cleaning, outlier detection, seasonal decomposition, and
> publication-quality visualisation. The `forecast` and `trend` packages add
> statistical time-series tools with minimal boilerplate. Tasks that require
> *making sense of the data* stay in R.

---

# Part 1 — Data Acquisition (Python)

## 1.1 Environment Setup

Install dependencies once (run in a terminal, not inside Quarto):

```bash
pip install earthengine-api geemap pandas numpy
```

Then authenticate with Google Earth Engine (also run once in a terminal):

```bash
earthengine authenticate
```

```{python}
#| label: py-imports
#| eval: false

import ee
import geemap
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime
import os

# Initialise Earth Engine.
# Replace 'your-project-id' with your GEE Cloud project.
# After running `earthengine authenticate` once, this call uses the cached
# credentials automatically.
ee.Initialize(project="your-project-id")

print("Earth Engine initialised successfully.")
```

## 1.2 Area of Interest

```{python}
#| label: py-aoi

# Coordinates given as (latitude, longitude) pairs.
# GEE's ee.Geometry.BBox expects (west, south, east, north) — i.e., lon/lat order.
SW_LAT, SW_LON = 31.935964, -109.083181
NE_LAT, NE_LON = 31.939848, -109.077395

aoi = ee.Geometry.BBox(
    west  = SW_LON,   # westernmost longitude
    south = SW_LAT,   # southernmost latitude
    east  = NE_LON,   # easternmost longitude
    north = NE_LAT,   # northernmost latitude
)

print(f"AOI: {SW_LON}, {SW_LAT}  →  {NE_LON}, {NE_LAT}")
print(f"Width  ≈ {abs(NE_LON - SW_LON) * 111_000 * np.cos(np.radians(NE_LAT)):.0f} m")
print(f"Height ≈ {abs(NE_LAT - SW_LAT) * 111_000:.0f} m")
```

## 1.3 Cloud Masking

All Landsat Collection 2 Level-2 (surface reflectance) products carry a
`QA_PIXEL` band whose bit flags identify cloud, cloud shadow, dilated cloud,
and snow/ice. We mask any pixel that is flagged on any of those bits.

```{python}
#| label: py-cloud-mask

def mask_clouds_l2(image):
    """
    Mask cloud and cloud-shadow pixels in a Landsat Collection-2 SR image.

    The QA_PIXEL band uses the Fmask algorithm (CFmask v2):
      Bit 1 — Dilated Cloud
      Bit 3 — Cloud
      Bit 4 — Cloud Shadow
      Bit 5 — Snow / Ice   (optional; uncomment if relevant)

    Keeping only pixels where ALL four bits are 0 gives a conservative but
    high-quality cloud-free mask.
    """
    qa = image.select("QA_PIXEL")
    dilated_cloud = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 1).eq(0)
    cloud         = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 3).eq(0)
    cloud_shadow  = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 4).eq(0)
    # snow_ice    = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 5).eq(0)  # uncomment for snow-prone areas

    mask = dilated_cloud.And(cloud).And(cloud_shadow)
    return image.updateMask(mask)
```

## 1.4 NDVI Calculation

NDVI = (NIR − Red) / (NIR + Red)

Band numbering differs between Landsat TM/ETM+ (4, 5, 7) and OLI (8, 9):

| Sensor family | Red band | NIR band |
|---------------|----------|----------|
| TM / ETM+     | SR_B3    | SR_B4    |
| OLI           | SR_B4    | SR_B5    |

```{python}
#| label: py-ndvi-fns

def add_ndvi_tm(image):
    """NDVI for Landsat 4, 5 (TM) and Landsat 7 (ETM+) — Collection 2 SR."""
    ndvi = image.normalizedDifference(["SR_B4", "SR_B3"]).rename("NDVI")
    return image.addBands(ndvi)


def add_ndvi_oli(image):
    """NDVI for Landsat 8 and 9 (OLI) — Collection 2 SR."""
    ndvi = image.normalizedDifference(["SR_B5", "SR_B4"]).rename("NDVI")
    return image.addBands(ndvi)
```

## 1.5 Assemble the Landsat Collection

We merge four Landsat missions into a single ImageCollection covering
1984–2025. Landsat 4 images are available but sparse; Landsat 5 is the
workhorse sensor from 1984 to 2012.

**Note on the 1980–1984 gap:** The only moderate-resolution orbital sensor
operational before Landsat 5's launch (March 1984) is Landsat 4 TM (1982–1983,
sparse) and Landsat 1–3 MSS (1972–1983, 60 m, no reliable SR product). NOAA
AVHRR begins in 1981 but at 8 km resolution — one pixel for the entire study
area — making pixel-scale NDVI meaningless. The practical earliest date for
reliable 30 m NDVI over this AOI is therefore **1984**.

```{python}
#| label: py-collections

START_DATE = "1984-01-01"
END_DATE   = "2025-12-31"

# ── Landsat 4 TM (1982–1993, Collection 2 SR)
l4 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LT04/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate(START_DATE, "1993-12-31")
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_tm)
)

# ── Landsat 5 TM (1984–2012)
l5 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LT05/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("1984-01-01", "2013-06-05")
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_tm)
)

# ── Landsat 7 ETM+ (1999–2021)
# The Scan Line Corrector (SLC) failed on 31 May 2003, leaving ~22 % data gaps
# per scene after that date. We still use L7 because it overlaps with L5 and
# L8, providing cross-calibration; SLC-off scenes are handled in the R
# cleaning step.
l7 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LE07/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("1999-01-01", "2021-12-31")
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_tm)
)

# ── Landsat 8 OLI (2013–present)
l8 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("2013-04-11", END_DATE)
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_oli)
)

# ── Landsat 9 OLI-2 (2021–present)
l9 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC09/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("2021-10-31", END_DATE)
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_oli)
)

# ── Merge into one collection, sorted by acquisition time
all_landsat = (
    l4.merge(l5).merge(l7).merge(l8).merge(l9)
    .select("NDVI")
    .sort("system:time_start")
)

count = all_landsat.size().getInfo()
print(f"Total scenes after cloud masking: {count}")
```

## 1.6 Extract Time Series as a Table

For a 580 × 440 m AOI we reduce each scene to the spatial mean NDVI. This
collapses the data to one row per date — ideal for long time-series analysis
and small enough to store as a CSV.

```{python}
#| label: py-extract

def extract_mean_ndvi(image):
    """
    Reduce a single-band NDVI image over the AOI to its mean value.
    Returns a Feature with date, mean NDVI, scene ID, and sensor name.
    """
    stats = image.reduceRegion(
        reducer   = ee.Reducer.mean(),
        geometry  = aoi,
        scale     = 30,      # Landsat native resolution
        maxPixels = 1e6,
    )

    # Attach metadata as properties so they survive the toDictionary() call
    return ee.Feature(None, {
        "date"   : image.date().format("YYYY-MM-dd"),
        "ndvi"   : stats.get("NDVI"),
        "scene_id": image.get("LANDSAT_SCENE_ID"),
        "sensor" : image.get("SPACECRAFT_ID"),
    })


# Map the extraction function over the full collection.
# .getInfo() pulls the results from the GEE servers — may take 1–5 minutes
# depending on the number of scenes.
features = all_landsat.map(extract_mean_ndvi).getInfo()

# Convert the GEE FeatureCollection structure to a flat list of dicts
records = [feat["properties"] for feat in features["features"]]
df_raw = pd.DataFrame(records)

print(f"Rows extracted : {len(df_raw)}")
print(df_raw.head())
```

## 1.7 Save Raw Data to Disk

```{python}
#| label: py-save

os.makedirs("data", exist_ok=True)
output_path = "data/ndvi_raw.csv"
df_raw.to_csv(output_path, index=False)
print(f"Saved → {output_path}")
```

---

# Part 2 — Data Cleaning & Analysis (R)

## 2.1 Load Packages

```{r}
#| label: r-packages

library(tidyverse)   # data manipulation + ggplot2
library(lubridate)   # date parsing and arithmetic
library(zoo)         # rolling statistics and irregular time series
library(forecast)    # STL decomposition, trend/seasonality extraction
library(ggridges)    # ridge plots for seasonal distributions
library(patchwork)   # combine ggplot2 panels
library(scales)      # axis formatting helpers
```

## 2.2 Load Raw Data

```{r}
#| label: r-load

raw <- read_csv("data/ndvi_raw.csv",
                col_types = cols(
                  date     = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
                  ndvi     = col_double(),
                  scene_id = col_character(),
                  sensor   = col_character()
                ))

glimpse(raw)
```

## 2.3 Initial Diagnostics

Before cleaning it is important to understand what the raw data look like —
how many scenes per sensor, the date range, and the distribution of NDVI values.

```{r}
#| label: r-diagnostics

# Scene counts by sensor
raw |>
  count(sensor, name = "n_scenes") |>
  arrange(sensor) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Scene count by sensor")

# Date range
cat(sprintf(
  "Date range : %s  →  %s\n",
  min(raw$date, na.rm = TRUE),
  max(raw$date, na.rm = TRUE)
))

# NDVI distribution
summary(raw$ndvi)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-raw-ts
#| fig-cap: "Raw NDVI time series coloured by sensor, before cleaning."

ggplot(raw, aes(x = date, y = ndvi, colour = sensor)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title   = "Raw Landsat NDVI — all scenes",
    x       = NULL,
    y       = "Mean NDVI",
    colour  = "Sensor",
    caption = "SE Arizona study area  |  30 m Landsat Collection 2 SR"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 2.4 Cleaning Steps

### Step 1 — Remove physically impossible NDVI values

NDVI is bounded [−1, 1] by definition; values outside [−0.2, 0.9] for a
vegetated semi-arid site almost certainly reflect residual cloud, cloud
shadow, instrument artefact, or a partially masked bad pixel.

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-range

NDVI_MIN <- -0.2   # below this = bare/water artefact or bad pixel
NDVI_MAX <-  0.9   # above this = implausibly high for semi-arid

step1 <- raw |>
  filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>                          # drop scenes with no valid pixels
  filter(between(ndvi, NDVI_MIN, NDVI_MAX))        # physical bounds filter

cat(sprintf(
  "Removed %d rows outside [%.1f, %.1f] (%.1f%% of raw)\n",
  nrow(raw) - nrow(step1),
  NDVI_MIN, NDVI_MAX,
  100 * (nrow(raw) - nrow(step1)) / nrow(raw)
))
```

### Step 2 — Flag Landsat 7 SLC-off scenes (post-31 May 2003)

After the Scan Line Corrector failure, ~22 % of each Landsat 7 scene is
missing (striped). The spatial mean over a small AOI may be biased if the
missing stripes happen to fall on vegetation. We flag these observations
so downstream analyses can optionally exclude them.

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-slc

SLC_FAIL_DATE <- as.Date("2003-06-01")   # 31 May failure, effective from June

step2 <- step1 |>
  mutate(
    slc_off = (sensor == "LANDSAT_7") & (date >= SLC_FAIL_DATE)
  )

cat(sprintf(
  "Flagged %d Landsat 7 SLC-off scenes (%.1f%% of step-1 data)\n",
  sum(step2$slc_off),
  100 * mean(step2$slc_off)
))
```

### Step 3 — Remove statistical outliers within each calendar month

Cloud masking is imperfect. A scene-level mean NDVI that is more than
3 × IQR below the 25th percentile or above the 75th percentile *within
the same calendar month* is likely a residual bad-pixel artefact.

Using a month-stratified fence (rather than a global one) avoids discarding
the genuinely high-NDVI observations that occur during the summer monsoon
(July–September).

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-outliers

step3 <- step2 |>
  mutate(month = month(date)) |>
  group_by(month) |>
  mutate(
    q25   = quantile(ndvi, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    q75   = quantile(ndvi, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    iqr   = q75 - q25,
    lower = q25 - 3 * iqr,
    upper = q75 + 3 * iqr,
    outlier = ndvi < lower | ndvi > upper
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!outlier) |>
  select(-q25, -q75, -iqr, -lower, -upper, -outlier)

cat(sprintf(
  "Removed %d monthly outliers (%.1f%% of step-2 data)\n",
  nrow(step2) - nrow(step3),
  100 * (nrow(step2) - nrow(step3)) / nrow(step2)
))
```

### Step 4 — De-duplicate same-date observations

Landsat path/row overlaps can produce two scenes on the same calendar day
(from adjacent orbits). We keep the observation with more valid pixels
(higher NDVI is a proxy for less cloud cover when values are otherwise
similar); where NDVI values are equal we keep the first alphabetically by
scene ID.

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-dedup

step4 <- step3 |>
  group_by(date) |>
  slice_max(ndvi, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  arrange(date)

cat(sprintf(
  "De-duplicated: %d → %d rows (removed %d)\n",
  nrow(step3), nrow(step4), nrow(step3) - nrow(step4)
))
```

### Step 5 — Save cleaned data

```{r}
#| label: r-save-clean

write_csv(step4, "data/ndvi_clean.csv")
cat("Saved → data/ndvi_clean.csv\n")
```

## 2.5 Cleaning Summary

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-summary

tibble(
  Step        = c("Raw", "Step 1 — range filter", "Step 2 — SLC flag", "Step 3 — outlier removal", "Step 4 — de-duplication"),
  `Row count` = c(nrow(raw), nrow(step1), nrow(step2), nrow(step3), nrow(step4)),
) |>
  mutate(`Rows removed` = lag(`Row count`, default = nrow(raw)) - `Row count`) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Data volume at each cleaning step")
```

---

# Part 3 — Exploratory Analysis (R)

## 3.1 Long-Term Time Series

```{r}
#| label: fig-clean-ts
#| fig-cap: "Cleaned NDVI time series with a 1-year rolling mean."

ndvi_clean <- read_csv("data/ndvi_clean.csv",
                       col_types = cols(date = col_date()))

# 1-year rolling mean (window = 23 observations ≈ 16-day cadence × 23)
ndvi_roll <- ndvi_clean |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(ndvi_roll = rollapply(ndvi, width = 23, FUN = mean,
                               fill = NA, align = "center", partial = TRUE))

ggplot(ndvi_roll, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = ndvi, colour = sensor), alpha = 0.25, size = 0.6) +
  geom_line(aes(y = ndvi_roll), colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1", name = "Sensor") +
  scale_x_date(breaks = "5 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(
    title   = "Landsat NDVI time series — SE Arizona study area (1984–2025)",
    subtitle = "Points: individual scenes  |  Line: 1-year rolling mean",
    x = NULL, y = "Mean NDVI"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 3.2 Seasonal Patterns (Ridge Plot)

The North American Monsoon drives a predictable summer green-up (July–
September) in this region. Ridge plots reveal whether the seasonal amplitude
has changed over the four-decade record.

```{r}
#| label: fig-seasonal-ridge
#| fig-cap: "Monthly NDVI distributions grouped by decade. Wider distributions and rightward shifts indicate higher vegetation density."

ndvi_clean |>
  mutate(
    month_name = factor(month.abb[month(date)], levels = month.abb),
    decade     = paste0(floor(year(date) / 10) * 10, "s")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ndvi, y = month_name, fill = decade)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.55, scale = 1.2, rel_min_height = 0.02) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma", direction = -1) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly NDVI distributions by decade",
    x = "Mean NDVI", y = NULL, fill = "Decade"
  ) +
  theme_ridges(font_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

## 3.3 Annual Monsoon-Season NDVI

Monsoon season (July–September) NDVI is a standard indicator of inter-annual
vegetation productivity in Chihuahuan/Sonoran Desert grasslands.

```{r}
#| label: fig-annual-monsoon
#| fig-cap: "Annual monsoon-season (JAS) mean NDVI with a linear trend line."

monsoon_annual <- ndvi_clean |>
  filter(month(date) %in% 7:9) |>
  group_by(year = year(date)) |>
  summarise(
    ndvi_mean = mean(ndvi, na.rm = TRUE),
    n         = n(),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n >= 3)  # require at least 3 scenes to compute a reliable seasonal mean

ggplot(monsoon_annual, aes(x = year, y = ndvi_mean)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4dac26", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title   = "Annual monsoon-season (July–September) mean NDVI",
    subtitle = "Bar = annual mean  |  Line = OLS trend ± 95% CI",
    x = "Year", y = "Mean NDVI (JAS)"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1985, 2025, 5)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

## 3.4 Long-Term Trend Test (Mann–Kendall)

The Mann–Kendall test is non-parametric, making it appropriate for NDVI data
which are often non-normal and may contain tied values.

```{r}
#| label: r-mk-test

# Use the annual monsoon means (one value per year) to avoid autocorrelation
# inflating significance in the raw scene-level time series.
if (!requireNamespace("trend", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("trend")
library(trend)

mk_result <- mk.test(monsoon_annual$ndvi_mean)

cat("Mann–Kendall trend test on annual monsoon NDVI\n")
cat(sprintf("  tau  = %.3f\n",  mk_result$estimates["tau"]))
cat(sprintf("  p    = %.4f\n",  mk_result$p.value))
cat(sprintf("  Trend direction: %s\n",
            ifelse(mk_result$estimates["tau"] > 0, "increasing", "decreasing")))
```

## 3.5 STL Decomposition

STL (Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess) separates the time series
into trend, seasonality, and residual components. We use a monthly-aggregated
series to feed STL, which requires a regular time axis.

```{r}
#| label: fig-stl
#| fig-cap: "STL decomposition of monthly mean NDVI into trend, seasonal, and residual components."

# Aggregate to monthly means to build a regular time series
monthly <- ndvi_clean |>
  mutate(ym = floor_date(date, "month")) |>
  group_by(ym) |>
  summarise(ndvi = mean(ndvi, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  # Fill any missing months with NA so ts() spacing is correct
  complete(ym = seq(min(ym), max(ym), by = "month")) |>
  arrange(ym)

# Interpolate short gaps (≤ 2 consecutive months) with linear interpolation
monthly <- monthly |>
  mutate(ndvi_interp = na.approx(ndvi, maxgap = 2, na.rm = FALSE))

# Convert to ts object (monthly, starting from first observation year/month)
start_yr  <- year(min(monthly$ym))
start_mo  <- month(min(monthly$ym))
ndvi_ts   <- ts(monthly$ndvi_interp,
                start     = c(start_yr, start_mo),
                frequency = 12)

# STL decomposition
stl_fit <- stl(ndvi_ts, s.window = "periodic", robust = TRUE,
               na.action = na.interp)

autoplot(stl_fit) +
  labs(
    title   = "STL decomposition of monthly mean NDVI",
    caption = "s.window = periodic  |  robust = TRUE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

```{r}
#| fold: true

sessionInfo()
```

> Landsat, MODIS, Sentinel, and others — without downloading raw rasters. The
> Python GEE API is the industry standard for large-scale remote sensing time
> series extraction, and `geemap` extends it with helpful utilities. Tasks that
> require *talking to the satellite archive* stay in Python.

> **Why R here?**
> Once the time series data land as a local CSV, R's `tidyverse` ecosystem
> (especially `dplyr`, `lubridate`, and `ggplot2`) offers expressive, readable
> pipelines for data cleaning, outlier detection, seasonal decomposition, and
> publication-quality visualisation. The `forecast` and `trend` packages add
> statistical time-series tools with minimal boilerplate. Tasks that require
> *making sense of the data* stay in R.

---

# Part 1 — Data Acquisition (Python)

## 1.1 Environment Setup

Install dependencies once (run in a terminal, not inside Quarto):

```bash
pip install earthengine-api geemap pandas numpy
```

Then authenticate with Google Earth Engine (also run once in a terminal):

```bash
earthengine authenticate
```

```{python}
#| label: py-imports
#| eval: false

import ee
import geemap
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from datetime import datetime
import os

# Initialise Earth Engine.
# Replace 'your-project-id' with your GEE Cloud project.
# After running `earthengine authenticate` once, this call uses the cached
# credentials automatically.
ee.Initialize(project="your-project-id")

print("Earth Engine initialised successfully.")
```

## 1.2 Area of Interest

```{python}
#| label: py-aoi

# Coordinates given as (latitude, longitude) pairs.
# GEE's ee.Geometry.BBox expects (west, south, east, north) — i.e., lon/lat order.
SW_LAT, SW_LON = 31.935964, -109.083181
NE_LAT, NE_LON = 31.939848, -109.077395

aoi = ee.Geometry.BBox(
    west  = SW_LON,   # westernmost longitude
    south = SW_LAT,   # southernmost latitude
    east  = NE_LON,   # easternmost longitude
    north = NE_LAT,   # northernmost latitude
)

print(f"AOI: {SW_LON}, {SW_LAT}  →  {NE_LON}, {NE_LAT}")
print(f"Width  ≈ {abs(NE_LON - SW_LON) * 111_000 * np.cos(np.radians(NE_LAT)):.0f} m")
print(f"Height ≈ {abs(NE_LAT - SW_LAT) * 111_000:.0f} m")
```

## 1.3 Cloud Masking

All Landsat Collection 2 Level-2 (surface reflectance) products carry a
`QA_PIXEL` band whose bit flags identify cloud, cloud shadow, dilated cloud,
and snow/ice. We mask any pixel that is flagged on any of those bits.

```{python}
#| label: py-cloud-mask

def mask_clouds_l2(image):
    """
    Mask cloud and cloud-shadow pixels in a Landsat Collection-2 SR image.

    The QA_PIXEL band uses the Fmask algorithm (CFmask v2):
      Bit 1 — Dilated Cloud
      Bit 3 — Cloud
      Bit 4 — Cloud Shadow
      Bit 5 — Snow / Ice   (optional; uncomment if relevant)

    Keeping only pixels where ALL four bits are 0 gives a conservative but
    high-quality cloud-free mask.
    """
    qa = image.select("QA_PIXEL")
    dilated_cloud = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 1).eq(0)
    cloud         = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 3).eq(0)
    cloud_shadow  = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 4).eq(0)
    # snow_ice    = qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 5).eq(0)  # uncomment for snow-prone areas

    mask = dilated_cloud.And(cloud).And(cloud_shadow)
    return image.updateMask(mask)
```

## 1.4 NDVI Calculation

NDVI = (NIR − Red) / (NIR + Red)

Band numbering differs between Landsat TM/ETM+ (4, 5, 7) and OLI (8, 9):

| Sensor family | Red band | NIR band |
|---------------|----------|----------|
| TM / ETM+     | SR_B3    | SR_B4    |
| OLI           | SR_B4    | SR_B5    |

```{python}
#| label: py-ndvi-fns

def add_ndvi_tm(image):
    """NDVI for Landsat 4, 5 (TM) and Landsat 7 (ETM+) — Collection 2 SR."""
    ndvi = image.normalizedDifference(["SR_B4", "SR_B3"]).rename("NDVI")
    return image.addBands(ndvi)


def add_ndvi_oli(image):
    """NDVI for Landsat 8 and 9 (OLI) — Collection 2 SR."""
    ndvi = image.normalizedDifference(["SR_B5", "SR_B4"]).rename("NDVI")
    return image.addBands(ndvi)
```

## 1.5 Assemble the Landsat Collection

We merge four Landsat missions into a single ImageCollection covering
1984–2025. Landsat 4 images are available but sparse; Landsat 5 is the
workhorse sensor from 1984 to 2012.

**Note on the 1980–1984 gap:** The only moderate-resolution orbital sensor
operational before Landsat 5's launch (March 1984) is Landsat 4 TM (1982–1983,
sparse) and Landsat 1–3 MSS (1972–1983, 60 m, no reliable SR product). NOAA
AVHRR begins in 1981 but at 8 km resolution — one pixel for the entire study
area — making pixel-scale NDVI meaningless. The practical earliest date for
reliable 30 m NDVI over this AOI is therefore **1984**.

```{python}
#| label: py-collections

START_DATE = "1984-01-01"
END_DATE   = "2025-12-31"

# ── Landsat 4 TM (1982–1993, Collection 2 SR)
l4 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LT04/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate(START_DATE, "1993-12-31")
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_tm)
)

# ── Landsat 5 TM (1984–2012)
l5 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LT05/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("1984-01-01", "2013-06-05")
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_tm)
)

# ── Landsat 7 ETM+ (1999–2021)
# The Scan Line Corrector (SLC) failed on 31 May 2003, leaving ~22 % data gaps
# per scene after that date. We still use L7 because it overlaps with L5 and
# L8, providing cross-calibration; SLC-off scenes are handled in the R
# cleaning step.
l7 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LE07/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("1999-01-01", "2021-12-31")
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_tm)
)

# ── Landsat 8 OLI (2013–present)
l8 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("2013-04-11", END_DATE)
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_oli)
)

# ── Landsat 9 OLI-2 (2021–present)
l9 = (
    ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC09/C02/T1_L2")
    .filterBounds(aoi)
    .filterDate("2021-10-31", END_DATE)
    .map(mask_clouds_l2)
    .map(add_ndvi_oli)
)

# ── Merge into one collection, sorted by acquisition time
all_landsat = (
    l4.merge(l5).merge(l7).merge(l8).merge(l9)
    .select("NDVI")
    .sort("system:time_start")
)

count = all_landsat.size().getInfo()
print(f"Total scenes after cloud masking: {count}")
```

## 1.6 Extract Time Series as a Table

For a 580 × 440 m AOI we reduce each scene to the spatial mean NDVI. This
collapses the data to one row per date — ideal for long time-series analysis
and small enough to store as a CSV.

```{python}
#| label: py-extract

def extract_mean_ndvi(image):
    """
    Reduce a single-band NDVI image over the AOI to its mean value.
    Returns a Feature with date, mean NDVI, scene ID, and sensor name.
    """
    stats = image.reduceRegion(
        reducer   = ee.Reducer.mean(),
        geometry  = aoi,
        scale     = 30,      # Landsat native resolution
        maxPixels = 1e6,
    )

    # Attach metadata as properties so they survive the toDictionary() call
    return ee.Feature(None, {
        "date"   : image.date().format("YYYY-MM-dd"),
        "ndvi"   : stats.get("NDVI"),
        "scene_id": image.get("LANDSAT_SCENE_ID"),
        "sensor" : image.get("SPACECRAFT_ID"),
    })


# Map the extraction function over the full collection.
# .getInfo() pulls the results from the GEE servers — may take 1–5 minutes
# depending on the number of scenes.
features = all_landsat.map(extract_mean_ndvi).getInfo()

# Convert the GEE FeatureCollection structure to a flat list of dicts
records = [feat["properties"] for feat in features["features"]]
df_raw = pd.DataFrame(records)

print(f"Rows extracted : {len(df_raw)}")
print(df_raw.head())
```

## 1.7 Save Raw Data to Disk

```{python}
#| label: py-save

os.makedirs("data", exist_ok=True)
output_path = "data/ndvi_raw.csv"
df_raw.to_csv(output_path, index=False)
print(f"Saved → {output_path}")
```

---

# Part 2 — Data Cleaning & Analysis (R)

## 2.1 Load Packages

```{r}
#| label: r-packages

library(tidyverse)   # data manipulation + ggplot2
library(lubridate)   # date parsing and arithmetic
library(zoo)         # rolling statistics and irregular time series
library(forecast)    # STL decomposition, trend/seasonality extraction
library(ggridges)    # ridge plots for seasonal distributions
library(patchwork)   # combine ggplot2 panels
library(scales)      # axis formatting helpers
```

## 2.2 Load Raw Data

```{r}
#| label: r-load

raw <- read_csv("data/ndvi_raw.csv",
                col_types = cols(
                  date     = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
                  ndvi     = col_double(),
                  scene_id = col_character(),
                  sensor   = col_character()
                ))

glimpse(raw)
```

## 2.3 Initial Diagnostics

Before cleaning it is important to understand what the raw data look like —
how many scenes per sensor, the date range, and the distribution of NDVI values.

```{r}
#| label: r-diagnostics

# Scene counts by sensor
raw |>
  count(sensor, name = "n_scenes") |>
  arrange(sensor) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Scene count by sensor")

# Date range
cat(sprintf(
  "Date range : %s  →  %s\n",
  min(raw$date, na.rm = TRUE),
  max(raw$date, na.rm = TRUE)
))

# NDVI distribution
summary(raw$ndvi)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-raw-ts
#| fig-cap: "Raw NDVI time series coloured by sensor, before cleaning."

ggplot(raw, aes(x = date, y = ndvi, colour = sensor)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title   = "Raw Landsat NDVI — all scenes",
    x       = NULL,
    y       = "Mean NDVI",
    colour  = "Sensor",
    caption = "SE Arizona study area  |  30 m Landsat Collection 2 SR"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 2.4 Cleaning Steps

### Step 1 — Remove physically impossible NDVI values

NDVI is bounded [−1, 1] by definition; values outside [−0.2, 0.9] for a
vegetated semi-arid site almost certainly reflect residual cloud, cloud
shadow, instrument artefact, or a partially masked bad pixel.

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-range

NDVI_MIN <- -0.2   # below this = bare/water artefact or bad pixel
NDVI_MAX <-  0.9   # above this = implausibly high for semi-arid

step1 <- raw |>
  filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>                          # drop scenes with no valid pixels
  filter(between(ndvi, NDVI_MIN, NDVI_MAX))        # physical bounds filter

cat(sprintf(
  "Removed %d rows outside [%.1f, %.1f] (%.1f%% of raw)\n",
  nrow(raw) - nrow(step1),
  NDVI_MIN, NDVI_MAX,
  100 * (nrow(raw) - nrow(step1)) / nrow(raw)
))
```

### Step 2 — Flag Landsat 7 SLC-off scenes (post-31 May 2003)

After the Scan Line Corrector failure, ~22 % of each Landsat 7 scene is
missing (striped). The spatial mean over a small AOI may be biased if the
missing stripes happen to fall on vegetation. We flag these observations
so downstream analyses can optionally exclude them.

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-slc

SLC_FAIL_DATE <- as.Date("2003-06-01")   # 31 May failure, effective from June

step2 <- step1 |>
  mutate(
    slc_off = (sensor == "LANDSAT_7") & (date >= SLC_FAIL_DATE)
  )

cat(sprintf(
  "Flagged %d Landsat 7 SLC-off scenes (%.1f%% of step-1 data)\n",
  sum(step2$slc_off),
  100 * mean(step2$slc_off)
))
```

### Step 3 — Remove statistical outliers within each calendar month

Cloud masking is imperfect. A scene-level mean NDVI that is more than
3 × IQR below the 25th percentile or above the 75th percentile *within
the same calendar month* is likely a residual bad-pixel artefact.

Using a month-stratified fence (rather than a global one) avoids discarding
the genuinely high-NDVI observations that occur during the summer monsoon
(July–September).

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-outliers

step3 <- step2 |>
  mutate(month = month(date)) |>
  group_by(month) |>
  mutate(
    q25   = quantile(ndvi, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    q75   = quantile(ndvi, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    iqr   = q75 - q25,
    lower = q25 - 3 * iqr,
    upper = q75 + 3 * iqr,
    outlier = ndvi < lower | ndvi > upper
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!outlier) |>
  select(-q25, -q75, -iqr, -lower, -upper, -outlier)

cat(sprintf(
  "Removed %d monthly outliers (%.1f%% of step-2 data)\n",
  nrow(step2) - nrow(step3),
  100 * (nrow(step2) - nrow(step3)) / nrow(step2)
))
```

### Step 4 — De-duplicate same-date observations

Landsat path/row overlaps can produce two scenes on the same calendar day
(from adjacent orbits). We keep the observation with more valid pixels
(higher NDVI is a proxy for less cloud cover when values are otherwise
similar); where NDVI values are equal we keep the first alphabetically by
scene ID.

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-dedup

step4 <- step3 |>
  group_by(date) |>
  slice_max(ndvi, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  arrange(date)

cat(sprintf(
  "De-duplicated: %d → %d rows (removed %d)\n",
  nrow(step3), nrow(step4), nrow(step3) - nrow(step4)
))
```

### Step 5 — Save cleaned data

```{r}
#| label: r-save-clean

write_csv(step4, "data/ndvi_clean.csv")
cat("Saved → data/ndvi_clean.csv\n")
```

## 2.5 Cleaning Summary

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-summary

tibble(
  Step        = c("Raw", "Step 1 — range filter", "Step 2 — SLC flag", "Step 3 — outlier removal", "Step 4 — de-duplication"),
  `Row count` = c(nrow(raw), nrow(step1), nrow(step2), nrow(step3), nrow(step4)),
) |>
  mutate(`Rows removed` = lag(`Row count`, default = nrow(raw)) - `Row count`) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Data volume at each cleaning step")
```

---

# Part 3 — Exploratory Analysis (R)

## 3.1 Long-Term Time Series

```{r}
#| label: fig-clean-ts
#| fig-cap: "Cleaned NDVI time series with a 1-year rolling mean."

ndvi_clean <- read_csv("data/ndvi_clean.csv",
                       col_types = cols(date = col_date()))

# 1-year rolling mean (window = 23 observations ≈ 16-day cadence × 23)
ndvi_roll <- ndvi_clean |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(ndvi_roll = rollapply(ndvi, width = 23, FUN = mean,
                               fill = NA, align = "center", partial = TRUE))

ggplot(ndvi_roll, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = ndvi, colour = sensor), alpha = 0.25, size = 0.6) +
  geom_line(aes(y = ndvi_roll), colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1", name = "Sensor") +
  scale_x_date(breaks = "5 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(
    title   = "Landsat NDVI time series — SE Arizona study area (1984–2025)",
    subtitle = "Points: individual scenes  |  Line: 1-year rolling mean",
    x = NULL, y = "Mean NDVI"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 3.2 Seasonal Patterns (Ridge Plot)

The North American Monsoon drives a predictable summer green-up (July–
September) in this region. Ridge plots reveal whether the seasonal amplitude
has changed over the four-decade record.

```{r}
#| label: fig-seasonal-ridge
#| fig-cap: "Monthly NDVI distributions grouped by decade. Wider distributions and rightward shifts indicate higher vegetation density."

ndvi_clean |>
  mutate(
    month_name = factor(month.abb[month(date)], levels = month.abb),
    decade     = paste0(floor(year(date) / 10) * 10, "s")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ndvi, y = month_name, fill = decade)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.55, scale = 1.2, rel_min_height = 0.02) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma", direction = -1) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly NDVI distributions by decade",
    x = "Mean NDVI", y = NULL, fill = "Decade"
  ) +
  theme_ridges(font_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

## 3.3 Annual Monsoon-Season NDVI

Monsoon season (July–September) NDVI is a standard indicator of inter-annual
vegetation productivity in Chihuahuan/Sonoran Desert grasslands.

```{r}
#| label: fig-annual-monsoon
#| fig-cap: "Annual monsoon-season (JAS) mean NDVI with a linear trend line."

monsoon_annual <- ndvi_clean |>
  filter(month(date) %in% 7:9) |>
  group_by(year = year(date)) |>
  summarise(
    ndvi_mean = mean(ndvi, na.rm = TRUE),
    n         = n(),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n >= 3)  # require at least 3 scenes to compute a reliable seasonal mean

ggplot(monsoon_annual, aes(x = year, y = ndvi_mean)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4dac26", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title   = "Annual monsoon-season (July–September) mean NDVI",
    subtitle = "Bar = annual mean  |  Line = OLS trend ± 95% CI",
    x = "Year", y = "Mean NDVI (JAS)"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1985, 2025, 5)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

## 3.4 Long-Term Trend Test (Mann–Kendall)

The Mann–Kendall test is non-parametric, making it appropriate for NDVI data
which are often non-normal and may contain tied values.

```{r}
#| label: r-mk-test

# Use the annual monsoon means (one value per year) to avoid autocorrelation
# inflating significance in the raw scene-level time series.
if (!requireNamespace("trend", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("trend")
library(trend)

mk_result <- mk.test(monsoon_annual$ndvi_mean)

cat("Mann–Kendall trend test on annual monsoon NDVI\n")
cat(sprintf("  tau  = %.3f\n",  mk_result$estimates["tau"]))
cat(sprintf("  p    = %.4f\n",  mk_result$p.value))
cat(sprintf("  Trend direction: %s\n",
            ifelse(mk_result$estimates["tau"] > 0, "increasing", "decreasing")))
```

## 3.5 STL Decomposition

STL (Seasonal and Trend decomposition using Loess) separates the time series
into trend, seasonality, and residual components. We use a monthly-aggregated
series to feed STL, which requires a regular time axis.

```{r}
#| label: fig-stl
#| fig-cap: "STL decomposition of monthly mean NDVI into trend, seasonal, and residual components."

# Aggregate to monthly means to build a regular time series
monthly <- ndvi_clean |>
  mutate(ym = floor_date(date, "month")) |>
  group_by(ym) |>
  summarise(ndvi = mean(ndvi, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  # Fill any missing months with NA so ts() spacing is correct
  complete(ym = seq(min(ym), max(ym), by = "month")) |>
  arrange(ym)

# Interpolate short gaps (≤ 2 consecutive months) with linear interpolation
monthly <- monthly |>
  mutate(ndvi_interp = na.approx(ndvi, maxgap = 2, na.rm = FALSE))

# Convert to ts object (monthly, starting from first observation year/month)
start_yr  <- year(min(monthly$ym))
start_mo  <- month(min(monthly$ym))
ndvi_ts   <- ts(monthly$ndvi_interp,
                start     = c(start_yr, start_mo),
                frequency = 12)

# STL decomposition
stl_fit <- stl(ndvi_ts, s.window = "periodic", robust = TRUE,
               na.action = na.interp)

autoplot(stl_fit) +
  labs(
    title   = "STL decomposition of monthly mean NDVI",
    caption = "s.window = periodic  |  robust = TRUE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

content.text:
The file `ndvi_analysis.qmd` is ready. Here's a summary of what it does and why:

---

## Document Structure

### Python (Parts 1) — Data Acquisition via Google Earth Engine
**Why Python:** GEE's Python client (`earthengine-api`) is the industry standard for accessing petabytes of satellite imagery without downloading raw rasters. There's no equivalent R client.

- **AOI definition** — converts your (lat, lon) corners to GEE's `BBox(west, south, east, north)` format
- **Cloud masking** — uses the `QA_PIXEL` Fmask bits (dilated cloud, cloud, cloud shadow) from Landsat Collection 2 SR
- **NDVI calculation** — separate functions for TM/ETM+ (Bands 3/4) vs. OLI (Bands 4/5) due to band renumbering between sensor families
- **Collection assembly** — merges Landsat 4, 5, 7, 8, and 9 into one timeline (1984–2025)
- **Extraction** — reduces each scene to a spatial mean over the AOI and exports as `data/ndvi_raw.csv`

> **Note on the 1980–1984 gap:** Landsat 5 launched March 1984. AVHRR (1981+) only has 8 km pixels — one pixel for your entire study area — making it unsuitable for a 580 × 440 m site.

### R (Parts 2–3) — Cleaning & Analysis
**Why R:** `tidyverse` + `ggplot2` + `forecast` provide a more expressive pipeline for data science than Python equivalents.

**Cleaning steps:**
1. Drop `NA` NDVI (scenes with no valid pixels after masking)
2. Physical bounds filter `[-0.2, 0.9]`
3. Flag Landsat 7 SLC-off scenes (post-2003-05-31)
4. Month-stratified 3×IQR outlier removal (preserves monsoon-season highs)
5. De-duplicate same-date scenes from path/row overlaps

**Analysis:**
- Long-term time series with 1-year rolling mean
- Seasonal ridge plots by decade
- Annual monsoon-season (JAS) mean NDVI + linear trend
- Mann–Kendall non-parametric trend test
- STL seasonal decomposition

---

**To run:** set `eval: true` in the YAML front matter after configuring GEE credentials, then `quarto render ndvi_analysis.qmd`.

message.content: Do I need a Google Earth Engine account?

content.text:
Yes. GEE requires a free account and a Google Cloud project. Here's what you need:

1. **Sign up** at [earthengine.google.com](https://earthengine.google.com) — free for non-commercial use
2. **Create a Google Cloud project** (also free) — GEE now requires one for the `ee.Initialize(project="...")` call
3. **Authenticate once** in your terminal: `earthengine authenticate`

---

## Alternatives if you'd rather avoid GEE

If you want to skip the account setup, there are two good options:

**Option A — Microsoft Planetary Computer** (free, no account required for public data)
Uses `pystac-client` + `planetary-computer` Python packages. Has all Landsat Collection 2 SR data. Slightly more verbose to query but no auth needed for read-only access.

**Option B — Stay entirely in R** using `rstac` + `gdalcubes`
The `rstac` package can query the Planetary Computer STAC API directly from R, and `gdalcubes` handles the raster time series without downloading full scenes. This would remove the Python dependency entirely.

---

Want me to rewrite the acquisition section using one of these alternatives so you don't need a GEE account?

message.content: What are the pros and cons to each?

content.text:
## Option A — Google Earth Engine (current approach)

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| Computation runs on Google's servers — no local storage or processing needed | Requires account + Google Cloud project setup |
| Best documentation and community for remote sensing time series | Authentication can be finicky, especially in non-interactive environments |
| `ee.Reducer.mean()` over an AOI is trivial — no raster downloads at all | GEE Python API changes periodically, breaking older scripts |
| Handles cross-sensor harmonization well | Overkill for a single small AOI |

---

## Option B — Microsoft Planetary Computer (Python)

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| No account required for public datasets | You download actual raster chips locally (small for your AOI, but still) |
| Same Landsat Collection 2 SR data as GEE | More boilerplate — you manage STAC queries, asset URLs, and `rioxarray` reads yourself |
| Stable, well-maintained API | Slower than GEE for large AOIs (irrelevant here) |
| Good for reproducibility — no credentials to manage | Less community documentation than GEE for time series workflows |

---

## Option C — Pure R (`rstac` + `gdalcubes`)

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| No Python required — single-language document | `gdalcubes` has a steeper learning curve than GEE or pandas |
| `rstac` queries the same Planetary Computer catalog, no account needed | GDAL must be correctly installed and configured (can be painful on macOS) |
| `gdalcubes` streams pixels on-demand — minimal local storage | Less mature ecosystem for this workflow than GEE Python |
| Keeps everything in one language, easier to maintain | Debugging raster I/O errors is harder than debugging a pandas DataFrame |

---

## Recommendation

**If you're comfortable with a one-time account setup → stick with GEE.** It's the least code, most reliable for a 40-year multi-sensor time series, and the one with the most Stack Overflow answers when things go wrong.

**If you want zero accounts and are comfortable in R → Option C.** Your cleaning and analysis is already in R, so a single-language document is cleaner.

**Option B is the middle ground** but gets you the downsides of both (Python *and* manual raster handling) without the upside of GEE's server-side processing.

Want me to rewrite the document with Option C (pure R)?

---
title: "NDVI Time Series Analysis (1984–2025)"
subtitle: "SE Arizona Study Area — Near Portal, Cochise County"
author: "Analysis Document"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  eval: false   # Global default — individual chunks override this
---

# Overview

This document finds, downloads, and cleans NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation
Index) data for a small study area in southeastern Arizona from the earliest
reliable satellite record through 2025. **Three independent data acquisition
paths are provided** — choose the one that fits your environment.

## Study Area

The bounding box is defined by two corners:

| Corner | Latitude  | Longitude   |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| NE     | 31.939848 | −109.077395 |
| SW     | 31.935964 | −109.083181 |

The box spans roughly **580 m (E–W) × 440 m (N–S)**, placing it in the
Peloncillo Mountains straddling the Arizona/New Mexico border. Vegetation in
this Sky Island region transitions from Chihuahuan Desert scrub to oak woodland
at higher elevations, making long-term NDVI trends ecologically informative.

## Data Source

All three options draw from **Landsat Collection 2 Level-2 Surface Reflectance**
(30 m resolution), merging Landsat 4, 5, 7, 8, and 9 into a single time series.

> **Note on the 1980–1984 gap:** Landsat 5 launched March 1984. The only sensor
> available before that date is NOAA AVHRR (from 1981), which has 8 km resolution
> — a single pixel covering the entire study area. Pixel-scale NDVI is not
> meaningful at that resolution for a 580 × 440 m site. The practical earliest
> date for reliable 30 m NDVI here is therefore **1984**.

## Choosing an Acquisition Method {#sec-choose}

Three options are provided. **Activate exactly one** by setting its first chunk's
`eval:` flag to `true` before rendering.

| | Option A — GEE | Option B — Planetary Computer (Python) | Option C — Pure R |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Account needed** | Yes (free) | No | No |
| **Languages** | Python | Python | R only |
| **Processing location** | Google's servers | Local machine | Local machine |
| **Downloads rasters** | No | Yes (small clips) | Yes (small clips) |
| **Best for** | Large AOIs, long records | No-account Python workflow | R-only environment |
| **Main dependency** | `earthengine-api` | `pystac-client`, `rioxarray` | `rstac`, `gdalcubes` |

All three write identical output to `data/ndvi_raw.csv`, so the cleaning and
analysis sections that follow work regardless of which path was used.

---

## Language Rationale

> **Why Python for acquisition (Options A & B)?**
> The Google Earth Engine Python client (`earthengine-api`) is the
> industry-standard tool for large-scale remote sensing time series —
> server-side computation means no raster downloads. The Planetary Computer
> Python stack (`pystac-client` + `rioxarray`) is the natural Python interface
> to Microsoft's STAC catalog, and both ecosystems have far more documentation
> and community support than their R equivalents for these specific tasks.
> Option C demonstrates that R can do the same job, at the cost of slightly more
> boilerplate.

> **Why R for cleaning and analysis?**
> Once the data land as a CSV, R's `tidyverse` ecosystem (`dplyr`, `lubridate`,
> `ggplot2`) provides expressive, readable pipelines for cleaning, outlier
> detection, and visualisation. The `forecast` and `trend` packages add
> statistical time-series tools with minimal boilerplate.
> "Tasks that talk to the satellite archive stay in Python; tasks that make
> sense of the data stay in R."

---

# Part 0 — Shared Constants

These coordinate constants are defined in both Python and R so each section
can be run independently.

```{python}
#| label: py-constants
#| eval: false   # sourced by Options A and B — do not change

SW_LAT, SW_LON =  31.935964, -109.083181   # southwest corner (lat, lon)
NE_LAT, NE_LON =  31.939848, -109.077395   # northeast corner (lat, lon)

START_DATE = "1984-01-01"
END_DATE   = "2025-12-31"
```

```{r}
#| label: r-constants
#| eval: true   # always run — R cleaning section needs these

SW_LAT <- 31.935964; SW_LON <- -109.083181
NE_LAT <- 31.939848; NE_LON <- -109.077395

START_DATE <- "1984-01-01"
END_DATE   <- "2025-12-31"
```

---

# Part 1A — Option A: Google Earth Engine (Python) {#sec-gee}

> **Requires:** A free GEE account + Google Cloud project.
> Run `earthengine authenticate` once in a terminal before using this section.
> Install: `pip install earthengine-api geemap pandas numpy`
>
> **To activate this option:** change the first chunk below from `eval: false`
> to `eval: true`, then render.

Computation runs entirely on Google's servers — no rasters are downloaded.
This is the recommended option for reproducibility across large time ranges.

## 1A.1 Initialise Earth Engine

```{python}
#| label: gee-init
#| eval: false   # ← SET TO true TO USE THIS OPTION

import ee
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import os

# Replace 'your-project-id' with your GEE Cloud project ID.
# Credentials are read from the cache created by `earthengine authenticate`.
ee.Initialize(project="your-project-id")
print("Earth Engine initialised.")
```

## 1A.2 Area of Interest

```{python}
#| label: gee-aoi
#| eval: false

# GEE BBox expects (west, south, east, north) — longitude before latitude.
aoi = ee.Geometry.BBox(
    west  = SW_LON,
    south = SW_LAT,
    east  = NE_LON,
    north = NE_LAT,
)
```

## 1A.3 Cloud Masking

Landsat Collection 2 Level-2 products carry a `QA_PIXEL` band generated by the
CFmask v2 (Fmask) algorithm. Each bit indicates a specific condition:

| Bit | Meaning         |
|-----|-----------------|
| 1   | Dilated cloud   |
| 3   | Cloud           |
| 4   | Cloud shadow    |

We retain only pixels where all three bits are zero.

```{python}
#| label: gee-cloud-mask
#| eval: false

def mask_clouds_l2(image):
    """Mask cloud/shadow pixels in a Landsat C2 SR image using QA_PIXEL bits."""
    qa = image.select("QA_PIXEL")
    clear = (
        qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 1).eq(0)   # dilated cloud
         .And(qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 3).eq(0))  # cloud
         .And(qa.bitwiseAnd(1 << 4).eq(0))  # cloud shadow
    )
    return image.updateMask(clear)
```

## 1A.4 NDVI Functions

NDVI = (NIR − Red) / (NIR + Red). Band numbering differs between sensor
families — TM/ETM+ use Bands 3/4, OLI uses Bands 4/5.

```{python}
#| label: gee-ndvi
#| eval: false

def add_ndvi_tm(image):
    """NDVI for Landsat 4, 5 (TM) and 7 (ETM+)."""
    return image.addBands(
        image.normalizedDifference(["SR_B4", "SR_B3"]).rename("NDVI")
    )

def add_ndvi_oli(image):
    """NDVI for Landsat 8 and 9 (OLI / OLI-2)."""
    return image.addBands(
        image.normalizedDifference(["SR_B5", "SR_B4"]).rename("NDVI")
    )
```

## 1A.5 Assemble Collections

```{python}
#| label: gee-collections
#| eval: false

# Landsat 4 TM (1982–1993) — sparse coverage
l4 = (ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LT04/C02/T1_L2")
      .filterBounds(aoi).filterDate(START_DATE, "1993-12-31")
      .map(mask_clouds_l2).map(add_ndvi_tm))

# Landsat 5 TM (1984–2012) — primary sensor for the 1980s–2000s
l5 = (ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LT05/C02/T1_L2")
      .filterBounds(aoi).filterDate("1984-01-01", "2013-06-05")
      .map(mask_clouds_l2).map(add_ndvi_tm))

# Landsat 7 ETM+ (1999–2021)
# ⚠ The Scan Line Corrector (SLC) failed 31 May 2003, leaving ~22% data gaps
# per scene. We include post-SLC scenes but flag them for the cleaning step.
l7 = (ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LE07/C02/T1_L2")
      .filterBounds(aoi).filterDate("1999-01-01", "2021-12-31")
      .map(mask_clouds_l2).map(add_ndvi_tm))

# Landsat 8 OLI (2013–present)
l8 = (ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2")
      .filterBounds(aoi).filterDate("2013-04-11", END_DATE)
      .map(mask_clouds_l2).map(add_ndvi_oli))

# Landsat 9 OLI-2 (2021–present)
l9 = (ee.ImageCollection("LANDSAT/LC09/C02/T1_L2")
      .filterBounds(aoi).filterDate("2021-10-31", END_DATE)
      .map(mask_clouds_l2).map(add_ndvi_oli))

all_landsat = (l4.merge(l5).merge(l7).merge(l8).merge(l9)
               .select("NDVI").sort("system:time_start"))

print(f"Total scenes after cloud masking: {all_landsat.size().getInfo()}")
```

## 1A.6 Extract Time Series

```{python}
#| label: gee-extract
#| eval: false

def extract_mean_ndvi(image):
    """Reduce one scene to spatial mean NDVI over the AOI."""
    stats = image.reduceRegion(
        reducer   = ee.Reducer.mean(),
        geometry  = aoi,
        scale     = 30,
        maxPixels = 1e6,
    )
    return ee.Feature(None, {
        "date"    : image.date().format("YYYY-MM-dd"),
        "ndvi"    : stats.get("NDVI"),
        "scene_id": image.get("LANDSAT_SCENE_ID"),
        "sensor"  : image.get("SPACECRAFT_ID"),
    })

features = all_landsat.map(extract_mean_ndvi).getInfo()
records  = [f["properties"] for f in features["features"]]
df       = pd.DataFrame(records)

print(f"Rows extracted: {len(df)}")
print(df.head())
```

## 1A.7 Save to Disk

```{python}
#| label: gee-save
#| eval: false

os.makedirs("data", exist_ok=True)
df.to_csv("data/ndvi_raw.csv", index=False)
print("Saved → data/ndvi_raw.csv")
```

---

# Part 1B — Option B: Microsoft Planetary Computer (Python) {#sec-pc}

> **Requires:** No account. Data is publicly accessible.
> Install: `pip install pystac-client planetary-computer rioxarray rasterio numpy pandas`
>
> **To activate this option:** change the first chunk below from `eval: false`
> to `eval: true`, then render.

Planetary Computer hosts the same Landsat Collection 2 Level-2 data as GEE.
This option downloads small raster chips for the AOI and computes NDVI locally.
For a 580 × 440 m clip at 30 m resolution that is roughly 20 × 15 = 300 pixels
per band — negligible download size (~50 KB per scene).

## 1B.1 Imports

```{python}
#| label: pc-imports
#| eval: false   # ← SET TO true TO USE THIS OPTION

import pystac_client
import planetary_computer
import rioxarray as rxr
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import os
from tqdm import tqdm   # progress bar — install with: pip install tqdm
```

## 1B.2 Query the STAC Catalog

```{python}
#| label: pc-query
#| eval: false

# Open the Planetary Computer STAC API.
# sign_inplace automatically appends a SAS token to each asset URL.
catalog = pystac_client.Client.open(
    "https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1",
    modifier=planetary_computer.sign_inplace,
)

# Pre-filter to scenes with < 70% cloud cover reported in metadata.
# Fine-grained cloud masking happens per-pixel below.
search = catalog.search(
    collections = ["landsat-c2-l2"],
    bbox        = [SW_LON, SW_LAT, NE_LON, NE_LAT],
    datetime    = f"{START_DATE}/{END_DATE}",
    query       = {"eo:cloud_cover": {"lt": 70}},
    max_items   = None,   # retrieve all matching scenes
)

items = list(search.items())
print(f"Found {len(items)} scenes with < 70% cloud cover")
```

## 1B.3 Cloud-Mask Helper

```{python}
#| label: pc-cloud-fn
#| eval: false

def apply_cloud_mask(qa_array: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Return a boolean array (True = clear pixel) from a QA_PIXEL array.
    Identical logic to Option A — bits 1, 3, 4 indicate cloud conditions.
    """
    qa = qa_array.astype(np.int32)
    return (
        (qa & (1 << 1) == 0) &   # dilated cloud
        (qa & (1 << 3) == 0) &   # cloud
        (qa & (1 << 4) == 0)     # cloud shadow
    )
```

## 1B.4 Extract NDVI Per Scene

The TM/ETM+ vs. OLI band-naming difference means we must inspect
`platform` metadata before selecting the red and NIR assets.

```{python}
#| label: pc-extract
#| eval: false

# Band asset names per sensor family
BAND_MAP = {
    "landsat-4": ("SR_B3", "SR_B4"),
    "landsat-5": ("SR_B3", "SR_B4"),
    "landsat-7": ("SR_B3", "SR_B4"),   # TM/ETM+: red = B3, NIR = B4
    "landsat-8": ("SR_B4", "SR_B5"),   # OLI:     red = B4, NIR = B5
    "landsat-9": ("SR_B4", "SR_B5"),
}

bbox = [SW_LON, SW_LAT, NE_LON, NE_LAT]
records = []

for item in tqdm(items, desc="Processing scenes"):
    platform = item.properties.get("platform", "")
    if platform not in BAND_MAP:
        continue

    red_key, nir_key = BAND_MAP[platform]

    try:
        # Read only the small AOI window from the COG (Cloud-Optimised GeoTIFF).
        # rioxarray's clip_box issues a partial HTTP range request — it does NOT
        # download the full scene (~1 GB).
        red = rxr.open_rasterio(item.assets[red_key].href,  masked=True).squeeze()
        nir = rxr.open_rasterio(item.assets[nir_key].href,  masked=True).squeeze()
        qa  = rxr.open_rasterio(item.assets["QA_PIXEL"].href, masked=False).squeeze()

        red = red.rio.clip_box(*bbox, crs="EPSG:4326")
        nir = nir.rio.clip_box(*bbox, crs="EPSG:4326")
        qa  = qa.rio.clip_box(*bbox, crs="EPSG:4326")

        clear = apply_cloud_mask(qa.values)

        r = red.values.astype(float); r[~clear] = np.nan
        n = nir.values.astype(float); n[~clear] = np.nan

        ndvi_mean = float(np.nanmean((n - r) / (n + r + 1e-10)))

        if not np.isnan(ndvi_mean):
            records.append({
                "date"    : item.datetime.strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
                "ndvi"    : ndvi_mean,
                "scene_id": item.id,
                "sensor"  : platform.upper().replace("-", "_"),
            })

    except Exception as exc:
        print(f"  Skipped {item.id}: {exc}")

df = pd.DataFrame(records).sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
print(f"Extracted NDVI for {len(df)} scenes")
print(df.head())
```

## 1B.5 Save to Disk

```{python}
#| label: pc-save
#| eval: false

os.makedirs("data", exist_ok=True)
df.to_csv("data/ndvi_raw.csv", index=False)
print("Saved → data/ndvi_raw.csv")
```

---

# Part 1C — Option C: Pure R (`rstac` + `gdalcubes`) {#sec-r-only}

> **Requires:** No account. Data is publicly accessible.
> Install R packages: `install.packages(c("rstac", "gdalcubes", "sf", "terra", "tidyverse"))`
> GDAL ≥ 3.1 must be installed on your system (comes with most spatial R installs).
>
> **To activate this option:** change the first chunk below from `eval: false`
> to `eval: true`, then render.

This option demonstrates that the full acquisition pipeline is possible in R
alone. It queries the same Planetary Computer STAC API and uses `gdalcubes` to
stream small raster windows on demand — no Python required.

## 1C.1 Load Packages

```{r}
#| label: r-only-pkgs
#| eval: false   # ← SET TO true TO USE THIS OPTION

library(rstac)      # STAC API queries
library(gdalcubes)  # on-demand raster data cube processing
library(sf)         # spatial reference handling
library(terra)      # fallback raster I/O (used for QA masking)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)

# Enable parallel reads in gdalcubes (speeds up multi-scene processing)
gdalcubes_options(parallel = TRUE)
```

## 1C.2 Query the STAC Catalog

```{r}
#| label: r-only-query
#| eval: false

# rstac connects to any STAC-compliant API.
# items_fetch() paginates automatically to retrieve all results.
items_raw <- stac("https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/api/stac/v1") |>
  stac_search(
    collections = "landsat-c2-l2",
    bbox        = c(SW_LON, SW_LAT, NE_LON, NE_LAT),
    datetime    = paste(START_DATE, END_DATE, sep = "/"),
    limit       = 250   # page size; items_fetch() will page through all results
  ) |>
  get_request() |>
  items_fetch()

# Sign all asset URLs with a Planetary Computer SAS token so GDAL can read them
items_signed <- items_raw |>
  items_sign(sign_fn = sign_planetary_computer())

n_items <- length(items_signed$features)
cat(sprintf("Found %d scenes\n", n_items))
```

## 1C.3 Build Sensor-Stratified Image Collections

`gdalcubes` requires knowing which asset contains each band. Because band
numbers differ between TM/ETM+ (Landsat 4/5/7) and OLI (Landsat 8/9), we
build two image collections and process them separately, then combine the
resulting time series tables.

```{r}
#| label: r-only-collections
#| eval: false

# Helper: extract platform from a STAC item
get_platform <- function(item) item$properties$platform

# Separate by sensor family
is_tm  <- map_lgl(items_signed$features, ~ get_platform(.x) %in%
                    c("landsat-4", "landsat-5", "landsat-7"))
is_oli <- map_lgl(items_signed$features, ~ get_platform(.x) %in%
                    c("landsat-8", "landsat-9"))

features_tm  <- items_signed$features[is_tm]
features_oli <- items_signed$features[is_oli]

cat(sprintf("TM/ETM+ scenes : %d\n", length(features_tm)))
cat(sprintf("OLI scenes     : %d\n", length(features_oli)))

# Build gdalcubes image collections
# asset_names: which STAC asset keys to load
# band_names:  rename them to consistent internal names
make_collection <- function(features, red_asset, nir_asset) {
  if (length(features) == 0) return(NULL)
  stac_image_collection(
    features,
    asset_names = c(red_asset, nir_asset, "QA_PIXEL"),
    band_names  = c("red", "nir", "qa")
  )
}

col_tm  <- make_collection(features_tm,  "SR_B3", "SR_B4")
col_oli <- make_collection(features_oli, "SR_B4", "SR_B5")
```

## 1C.4 Define Data Cube View

A `cube_view` specifies the target spatial extent, CRS, resolution, and time
step. Setting `dt = "P1D"` gives one composite per calendar day; scenes on the
same day are aggregated by the chosen `aggregation` method.

```{r}
#| label: r-only-cube-view
#| eval: false

aoi_view <- cube_view(
  srs         = "EPSG:4326",
  extent      = list(
    left   = SW_LON, right  = NE_LON,
    bottom = SW_LAT, top    = NE_LAT,
    t0     = START_DATE, t1 = END_DATE
  ),
  dx          = 0.00027,   # ≈ 30 m in decimal degrees at this latitude
  dy          = 0.00027,
  dt          = "P1D",     # one time slice per calendar day
  aggregation = "median",  # median composite when multiple scenes fall on same day
  resampling  = "bilinear"
)
```

## 1C.5 Compute NDVI and Reduce to Spatial Mean

```{r}
#| label: r-only-ndvi
#| eval: false

# Cloud-mask expression: QA bits 1, 3, 4 — same logic as Options A & B.
# gdalcubes apply_pixel() accepts C-style expressions over band names.
CLOUD_MASK_EXPR <- "
  (((int)qa & (1 << 1)) == 0) &&
  (((int)qa & (1 << 3)) == 0) &&
  (((int)qa & (1 << 4)) == 0)
"

process_collection <- function(col, sensor_label) {
  if (is.null(col)) return(tibble())

  raster_cube(col, aoi_view) |>
    # Apply cloud mask: set red and nir to NA where not clear
    apply_pixel(
      c(
        sprintf("(%s) ? red : NAN", CLOUD_MASK_EXPR),
        sprintf("(%s) ? nir : NAN", CLOUD_MASK_EXPR)
      ),
      names = c("red_clear", "nir_clear")
    ) |>
    # Compute NDVI
    apply_pixel(
      "(nir_clear - red_clear) / (nir_clear + red_clear)",
      names = "ndvi"
    ) |>
    # Spatial mean per time step — collapses the small AOI to one value per day
    reduce_space("mean(ndvi)") |>
    as_tibble() |>
    rename(date = time, ndvi = ndvi) |>
    filter(!is.na(ndvi)) |>
    mutate(
      date   = as.Date(date),
      sensor = sensor_label
    )
}

ts_tm  <- process_collection(col_tm,  "LANDSAT_TM_ETM")
ts_oli <- process_collection(col_oli, "LANDSAT_OLI")

df_r <- bind_rows(ts_tm, ts_oli) |>
  arrange(date) |>
  # gdalcubes does not expose scene-level IDs; use date+sensor as surrogate
  mutate(scene_id = paste(date, sensor, sep = "_"))

cat(sprintf("Extracted %d observations\n", nrow(df_r)))
glimpse(df_r)
```

## 1C.6 Save to Disk

```{r}
#| label: r-only-save
#| eval: false

dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)
write_csv(df_r, "data/ndvi_raw.csv")
cat("Saved → data/ndvi_raw.csv\n")
```

---

# Part 2 — Data Cleaning (R)

This section reads `data/ndvi_raw.csv` regardless of which acquisition option
was used. All three options write the same four columns: `date`, `ndvi`,
`scene_id`, `sensor`.

## 2.1 Load Packages

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-pkgs
#| eval: true

library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(zoo)
library(knitr)
```

## 2.2 Load and Inspect Raw Data

```{r}
#| label: r-load
#| eval: true

raw <- read_csv(
  "data/ndvi_raw.csv",
  col_types = cols(
    date     = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
    ndvi     = col_double(),
    scene_id = col_character(),
    sensor   = col_character()
  )
)

glimpse(raw)

raw |>
  count(sensor, name = "n_scenes") |>
  kable(caption = "Scene count by sensor (raw)")

cat(sprintf(
  "Date range: %s  →  %s\n",
  min(raw$date, na.rm = TRUE),
  max(raw$date, na.rm = TRUE)
))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-raw-ts
#| eval: true
#| fig-cap: "Raw NDVI values before cleaning, coloured by sensor."

ggplot(raw, aes(x = date, y = ndvi, colour = sensor)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(title = "Raw Landsat NDVI — all scenes",
       x = NULL, y = "Mean NDVI", colour = "Sensor") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 2.3 Cleaning Pipeline

### Step 1 — Drop rows with no valid pixels

Some scenes return `NA` if the AOI was 100% cloud-covered after masking.

```{r}
#| label: r-step1
#| eval: true

step1 <- raw |> filter(!is.na(ndvi))
cat(sprintf("Step 1 — dropped %d NA rows\n", nrow(raw) - nrow(step1)))
```

### Step 2 — Physical bounds filter

NDVI is bounded [−1, 1] by definition. Values below −0.2 or above 0.9 for a
semi-arid shrubland almost always indicate residual cloud contamination,
instrument artefact, or snow/ice.

```{r}
#| label: r-step2
#| eval: true

NDVI_MIN <- -0.2
NDVI_MAX <-  0.9

step2 <- step1 |> filter(between(ndvi, NDVI_MIN, NDVI_MAX))
cat(sprintf(
  "Step 2 — removed %d rows outside [%.1f, %.1f]\n",
  nrow(step1) - nrow(step2), NDVI_MIN, NDVI_MAX
))
```

### Step 3 — Flag Landsat 7 SLC-off scenes

The Scan Line Corrector failed on 31 May 2003. Post-failure scenes have ~22%
striped data loss. For a small AOI the spatial mean may be biased if the
missing stripes happen to fall on vegetation. We flag rather than remove so
analysts can choose to exclude them.

```{r}
#| label: r-step3
#| eval: true

SLC_FAIL <- as.Date("2003-06-01")

step3 <- step2 |>
  mutate(slc_off = (sensor %in% c("LANDSAT_7", "LANDSAT_TM_ETM")) &
                    (date >= SLC_FAIL))

cat(sprintf(
  "Step 3 — flagged %d Landsat 7 SLC-off scenes (%.1f%%)\n",
  sum(step3$slc_off),
  100 * mean(step3$slc_off)
))
```

### Step 4 — Month-stratified outlier removal

Residual cloud contamination typically drives NDVI unusually low; smoke can
drive it high. A 3×IQR fence computed within each calendar month avoids
discarding the legitimately high NDVI values that occur during the summer
monsoon (July–September) — a global fence would treat monsoon-season peaks
as outliers.

```{r}
#| label: r-step4
#| eval: true

step4 <- step3 |>
  mutate(month = month(date)) |>
  group_by(month) |>
  mutate(
    q25     = quantile(ndvi, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    q75     = quantile(ndvi, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    iqr     = q75 - q25,
    outlier = ndvi < (q25 - 3 * iqr) | ndvi > (q75 + 3 * iqr)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |>
  filter(!outlier) |>
  select(-q25, -q75, -iqr, -outlier, -month)

cat(sprintf(
  "Step 4 — removed %d monthly outliers\n",
  nrow(step3) - nrow(step4)
))
```

### Step 5 — De-duplicate same-date observations

Path/row overlaps can produce two scenes on the same calendar day. We keep
the observation with the higher NDVI (a proxy for less cloud contamination
when values are otherwise similar).

```{r}
#| label: r-step5
#| eval: true

step5 <- step4 |>
  group_by(date) |>
  slice_max(ndvi, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  ungroup() |>
  arrange(date)

cat(sprintf(
  "Step 5 — de-duplicated: %d → %d rows\n",
  nrow(step4), nrow(step5)
))
```

## 2.4 Cleaning Summary

```{r}
#| label: r-clean-summary
#| eval: true

tibble(
  Step = c(
    "Raw", "1 — NA removal", "2 — range filter",
    "3 — SLC flag", "4 — outlier removal", "5 — de-duplication"
  ),
  `Row count`    = c(nrow(raw), nrow(step1), nrow(step2),
                     nrow(step3), nrow(step4), nrow(step5)),
) |>
  mutate(`Rows removed` = lag(`Row count`, default = nrow(raw)) - `Row count`) |>
  kable(caption = "Data volume at each cleaning step")
```

## 2.5 Save Cleaned Data

```{r}
#| label: r-save-clean
#| eval: true

dir.create("data", showWarnings = FALSE)
write_csv(step5, "data/ndvi_clean.csv")
cat("Saved → data/ndvi_clean.csv\n")
```

---

# Part 3 — Analysis & Visualisation (R)

## 3.1 Long-Term Time Series

```{r}
#| label: r-load-clean
#| eval: true

library(forecast)  # STL decomposition
library(ggridges)  # ridge plots
library(patchwork) # combine panels
library(scales)

ndvi_clean <- read_csv("data/ndvi_clean.csv",
                       col_types = cols(date = col_date()))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-clean-ts
#| eval: true
#| fig-cap: "Cleaned NDVI time series with a 1-year rolling mean."

ndvi_roll <- ndvi_clean |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(
    ndvi_roll = rollapply(ndvi, width = 23, FUN = mean,
                          fill = NA, align = "center", partial = TRUE)
  )

ggplot(ndvi_roll, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_point(aes(y = ndvi, colour = sensor), alpha = 0.25, size = 0.6) +
  geom_line(aes(y = ndvi_roll), colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1", name = "Sensor") +
  scale_x_date(breaks = "5 years", date_labels = "%Y") +
  labs(
    title    = "Landsat NDVI — SE Arizona (1984–2025)",
    subtitle = "Points: individual scenes  |  Line: 1-year rolling mean",
    x = NULL, y = "Mean NDVI"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

## 3.2 Seasonal Patterns by Decade

The North American Monsoon drives a predictable summer green-up (July–September).
Ridge plots reveal whether the seasonal amplitude or timing has shifted.

```{r}
#| label: fig-seasonal-ridge
#| eval: true
#| fig-cap: "Monthly NDVI distributions by decade. Rightward shifts indicate higher vegetation productivity."

ndvi_clean |>
  mutate(
    month_name = factor(month.abb[month(date)], levels = month.abb),
    decade     = paste0(floor(year(date) / 10) * 10, "s")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = ndvi, y = month_name, fill = decade)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.55, scale = 1.2, rel_min_height = 0.02) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma", direction = -1) +
  labs(title = "Monthly NDVI distributions by decade",
       x = "Mean NDVI", y = NULL, fill = "Decade") +
  theme_ridges(font_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

## 3.3 Annual Monsoon-Season NDVI

July–September (JAS) NDVI is a standard productivity index for Chihuahuan /
Sonoran Desert grasslands and the most ecologically meaningful seasonal window
in this region.

```{r}
#| label: fig-annual-monsoon
#| eval: true
#| fig-cap: "Annual monsoon-season (JAS) mean NDVI with a linear OLS trend."

monsoon_annual <- ndvi_clean |>
  filter(month(date) %in% 7:9) |>
  group_by(year = year(date)) |>
  summarise(ndvi_mean = mean(ndvi, na.rm = TRUE), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(n >= 3)   # need ≥ 3 scenes to compute a stable seasonal mean

ggplot(monsoon_annual, aes(x = year, y = ndvi_mean)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4dac26", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, colour = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1985, 2025, 5)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Annual monsoon-season (JAS) mean NDVI",
    subtitle = "Bar = annual mean  |  Line = OLS trend ± 95% CI",
    x = "Year", y = "Mean NDVI (JAS)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

## 3.4 Long-Term Trend Test (Mann–Kendall)

The Mann–Kendall test is non-parametric and robust to non-normal distributions
and tied values — appropriate for NDVI data. We apply it to the annual monsoon
means (one value per year) to avoid autocorrelation inflating significance in
the scene-level series.

```{r}
#| label: r-mk-test
#| eval: true

if (!requireNamespace("trend", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("trend")
library(trend)

mk <- mk.test(monsoon_annual$ndvi_mean)

cat("Mann–Kendall trend test on annual monsoon (JAS) NDVI\n")
cat(sprintf("  tau        = %+.3f\n", mk$estimates["tau"]))
cat(sprintf("  p-value    = %.4f\n",  mk$p.value))
cat(sprintf("  Direction  : %s\n",
    ifelse(mk$estimates["tau"] > 0, "increasing", "decreasing")))
cat(sprintf("  Significant at α=0.05: %s\n",
    ifelse(mk$p.value < 0.05, "YES", "no")))
```

## 3.5 STL Decomposition

STL (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess) separates the monthly mean NDVI
into trend, seasonal, and remainder components. Short gaps (≤ 2 months) are
interpolated before decomposition.

```{r}
#| label: fig-stl
#| eval: true
#| fig-cap: "STL decomposition of monthly mean NDVI into trend, seasonal cycle, and residuals."

# Aggregate to monthly means
monthly <- ndvi_clean |>
  mutate(ym = floor_date(date, "month")) |>
  group_by(ym) |>
  summarise(ndvi = mean(ndvi, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  complete(ym = seq(min(ym), max(ym), by = "month")) |>
  arrange(ym) |>
  mutate(ndvi_interp = na.approx(ndvi, maxgap = 2, na.rm = FALSE))

# Build regular ts object
ndvi_ts <- ts(
  monthly$ndvi_interp,
  start     = c(year(min(monthly$ym)), month(min(monthly$ym))),
  frequency = 12
)

stl_fit <- stl(ndvi_ts, s.window = "periodic", robust = TRUE,
               na.action = na.interp)

autoplot(stl_fit) +
  labs(
    title   = "STL decomposition of monthly mean NDVI",
    caption = "s.window = periodic  |  robust = TRUE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```
